coredata.dc:title: Selective and sensitive chemosensor for lead ions using fluorescent carbon dots prepared from chocolate by one-step hydrothermal method
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yongli
Zhou, Qingxiang
Li, Jing
Lei, Man
Yan, Xiuyi
coredata.dc:description: Lead is a well-known heavy metal element, and the resulted pollution has absorbed much more attention. It is important and also a great challenge to develop simple and effective methods for sensitive determination of lead ion (Pb2+) from water. Present study described a new chemosensor for the determination of lead ion based on water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots, which were prepared from chocolate using a simple and low-cost one-step hydrothermal method. The fluorescent carbon dots exhibited excitation-dependent behavior with the maximum excitation at 280nm. This novel chemosensor demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Pb2+ based on the efficient fluorescence quenching effect, which was ascribed to the special chelation between Pb2+ and hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots. The developed chemosensor resulted in a limit of detection as low as 12.7nM. In addition, the proposed method was validated with five real water samples, and good spiked recoveries were achieved. All these indicated that this new chemosensor had great prospect and would be a good alternative for the routine analysis of lead ion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent carbon dots
Hydrothermal method
Chemosensor
Lead
Chocolate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and fluorescence of the CDs
Optimization for the determination of Pb2+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs and its sensing mechanism to Pb2+.

(A) TEM image of CDs. (B) Size distribution histogram of CDs. (C) FTIR spectra of CDs. (D) UV–vis absorption (a) and FL (excitation spectrum: b, emission spectrum: c) spectra of the CDs. (E) FL spectra of the CDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths. (F) The stability of CDs.
The FL spectra and optimization. (A) The FL spectra of CDs and CDs-Pb2+. (B, C) The optimization of CDs concentration. (D) The optimization of incubation time. (E) The optimization of pH value.

Working curve. (A) The fluorescence response of the CDs upon the addition of different concentrations of Pb2+. (B) A linear correlation of (F0 −F)/F0 values versus the concentration of Pb2+ over the range from 0.033 to 1.67μM.
The stability of the reaction between CDs and Pb2+.

Selectivity of CDs for 0.1μM of Pb2+ over 0.5μM of other metal ions(the blue bar) and the mixture of 0.1μM of Pb2+ with 0.5μM of other metal ions (the red bar). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of different chemosensors for the detection of Pb2+.
Determination and spiked recoveries for real water samples.

head.ce:title: Selective and sensitive chemosensor for lead ions using fluorescent carbon dots prepared from chocolate by one-step hydrothermal method

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Pollution Control, China University of Petroleum Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Oil and Gas Pollution Control

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lead is a well-known heavy metal element, and the resulted pollution has absorbed much more attention. It is important and also a great challenge to develop simple and effective methods for sensitive determination of lead ion (Pb2+) from water. Present study described a new chemosensor for the determination of lead ion based on water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots, which were prepared from chocolate using a simple and low-cost one-step hydrothermal method. The fluorescent carbon dots exhibited excitation-dependent behavior with the maximum excitation at 280nm. This novel chemosensor demonstrated high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of Pb2+ based on the efficient fluorescence quenching effect, which was ascribed to the special chelation between Pb2+ and hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon dots. The developed chemosensor resulted in a limit of detection as low as 12.7nM. In addition, the proposed method was validated with five real water samples, and good spiked recoveries were achieved. All these indicated that this new chemosensor had great prospect and would be a good alternative for the routine analysis of lead ion.

ce:section.ce:para: The pollution of heavy metals has put a serious threat to the environment and human health, and it has become an increasingly important issue to efficiently monitor the concentration of toxic heavy metal ions in the environment [1–3]. Lead, one of the most common toxic heavy metals, is widely used in pigments, water pipes, storage batteries, anticorrosion coatings, alloys, etc and spreads in different areas of the environment by all ways. Meanwhile, Pb2+ is very easily accumulated in human nervous and cardiovascular systems when exposed to contaminated air and water sources owing to its non-biodegradability [4,5]. When the concentration of Pb2+ is higher than 5μM in blood, it can lead to anemia, reproductive dysfunction, nervous system dysfunction and developmental disorders, etc. Moreover, too high concentration of Pb2+ can even result in death [6–9]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop rapid and sensitive methods for the determination of Pb2+ at trace level.

Generally, there are many methods, which have been developed for the determination of Pb2+ such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [10], anodic stripping voltammetry (ASV) [11], inductive coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES), and inductive coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [12]. However, these methods usually require expensive and sophisticated instrumentation, time consumption and complex sample pretreatment procedures. In recent years, a variety of novel sensors have been exploited for the determination of Pb2+ based on imprinted polymer nanoparticles [13], thermosensitive tri-block copolymers [14], functionalized multiwalled carbon nanotubes [15] and magnetic mesoporous silica [16], DNAzyme [17–19], nobel metal-based nanoparticles such as Au [20,21] and Ag [22,23], and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [24,25]. However, these sensors needed multiple pre-treatment steps, rigorous synthetic conditions and some raw materials were very expensive. Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) earned size-tunable emission profiles and high fluorescence quantum yields, and have been developed as fluorescent sensors and successfully applied to determine Pb2+, yet they are severely toxic even at rather low concentration and in most cases the detection is carried out in organic solvents which limited their applications [26]. Hence, it is urgent to develop simple, rapid, low-cost, and reliable methods for sensitive and selective determination of Pb2+.

Recently, carbon dots (CD), a new member of fluorescent nanoparticles, have attracted tremendous interests from the researchers. CDs are usually spherical structure with the size below 10nm and exhibit strong and stable fluorescence. It is noted that they can be prepared by facile, simple, and rapid synthetic methods with cheap and available raw materials. Meanwhile, they have fascinating properties including outstanding water solubility, good photostability, super biocompatibility, and low toxicity [27–30] in contrast to organic dyes, quantum dots and noble metal nanoparticles, which make them be efficiently utilized for practical applications in chemosensors and biosensors. For instance, Zhai et al. used CDs as a novel biosensor for determination of Hg2+ in complex matrices [31]. Mohapatra et al. developed a chemosensor based on CDs for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution [32]. Zong et al. reported a facile one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize highly fluorescent CDs from pipe tobacco which was used to establish sensitive method for the determination of Cu2+ and l-cysteine in water [33]. Xu et al. synthesized S-doped CDs by a hydrothermal method using sodium thiosulfate and sodium citrate, which demonstrated outstanding sensitivity for Fe3+ [34], and Bai et al. developed a CDs-based fluorescence turn-on method for sensitive determination of DNA [35].

Nowadays, hydrothermal method has been one of the most commonly used methods to prepare CDs due to the advances of simplicity, easy to operate and low cost. The as-synthesized CDs are often functionalized with hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on their surfaces, which can be coordinated to metal ions and heavy ions [36,37]. In Present study, a simple hydrothermal method was adopted to synthesize water-soluble fluorescent CDs with an average particle size of 6.41nm with the chocolate as the precursor. The chocolate is inexpensive and easily available, and it is a good raw material for preparing water-soluble fluorescent CDs because it is rich in carbon and oxygen. More importantly, the as-prepared CDs could be used as a fluorescence sensor for sensitive and selective detection of Pb2+. The principle based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism was showed in Fig. 1 . Some critical parameters related to the detection such as the concentration of CDs, the incubation time, and pH of CDs solutions had been investigated and optimized to obtain the best conditions for the determination of Pb2+. To our knowledge, this is the first application of CDs from chocolate for the detection of Pb2+, and which provides a novel alternative method for sensitive detection of Pb2+.

Chocolate (Dove) was obtained from a local supermarket. Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and hydrochloric acid were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. Standard stock solutions of Pb2+ were prepared using Pb(NO3)2 with ultrapure water and HNO3. Other stock solutions of metal cations at 0.1mM were prepared in ultrapure water with the respective salts of Hg(NO3)2·2H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CuCl2, NaAsO2, NaHAsO4·12H2O, MnCl2, Zn(NO3)2·2H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Mg(NO3)2, NiCl2·6H2O, CdCl2, CoCl2·6H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, SnCl2·2H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, AgNO3, NaCl and KCl.

Five real water samples were obtained from Changping Park, Shahe River, North Moat River, Gaobeidian Reservoir (Beijing, China) and Chaoyang Electroplate Factory in Chaoyang City (Liaoning Province, China), respectively. All reagents were of analytical grade and without further purification, and ultrapure water was used throughout all the experiments.

Chocolate (3.0g) was dissolved in 20mL 10M NaOH solution. After vigorous stirring, the mixture was added into a 50mL polytetrafluoroethylene reaction kettle and reacted in an electroheat baking oven at 200°C for 8h. Then, the kettle was cooled to ambient temperature naturally, and a brown-yellow supernatant was obtained by centrifuging at 10,000rpm for 10min to remove the dark insoluble substances, and then adjusted to neutral with HCl and filtered with a 0.22μm filter membrane to get rid of the large particles. After that, the filtrate was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 18h. Finally, the obtained brown-yellow solution containing the CDs was collected and dried in an oven for 3h. A stock solution of CDs at a concentration of 1.2mgmL−1 was prepared by dispersing the appropriate amount of CDs in ultrapure water and stored at 4°C for subsequent experiments.

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded by a TU-1810 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PGeneral, China). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The morphologies of the samples were achieved by a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) using an acceleration voltage of 200kV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were performed on a Nicolet Magna-IR 750 spectrometer (Thermo, USA).

60μL CDs solution and different amounts of Pb2+ were added into a 5.0mL centrifuge tube, and the mixture was diluted to 3.0mL with ultrapure water and mixed completely. The fluorescence spectra were collected at room temperature subsequently from 320nm to 520nm with excitation wavelength at 280nm, and the slit widths of the excitation and emission were both 5nm. Nineteen metal cations such as Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, As3+, As5+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+ were chosen to assess the influence of co-existing ions on the fluorescence of CDs and evaluate the selectivity for Pb2+.

In order to evaluate the applicability and reliability, five real water samples were used for validating the CDs-based sensor for Pb2+. Five real water samples were filtered through a 0.22μm microporous membrane before use. The blank samples and spiked samples were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and fluorescence of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of CDs was shown in Fig. 2 A, which shows that the CDs had high dispersity and uniform spherical shapes, and lacked of crystalline structures. The particle size distribution of the as-prepared CDs was displayed in Fig. 2B by judging from 100 random particles, and it revealed that the average diameter of the obtained CDs was approximately 6.41nm. In addition, the surface functional groups of CDs were investigated by FTIR spectra which were shown in Fig. 2C. From Fig. 2C we can see that there were two typical peaks at 3425cm−1 and 1397cm−1, which were assigned to the stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of OH, respectively. The peaks at 1584cm−1 and 1090cm−1 indicated the existence of CC and CO, respectively. While the peak centered at 2948cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibrations of CH [38]. It is found that the as-prepared CDs are surrounded by OH groups which make the obtained CDs not only display excellent water solubility but also easily coordinate with some metal ions [39].

Fig. 2D shows the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the water-soluble CDs. It was observed that the CDs had a largely featureless profile absorption in the UV–vis range. Meanwhile, it can be clearly seen that the maximum emission intensity was centered at 354nm with an excitation wavelength of 280nm. Moreover, the synthesized CDs exhibited excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior which was similar as previous reports on CDs [40,41]. As shown in Fig. 2E, when the excitation wavelength increased from 260nm to 380nm, the fluorescence emission peak was obviously shifted to longer wavelength with variable fluorescence intensity which may be attributed to the different particle sizes or various surface energy traps of CDs [42,43]. The fluorescent stability is an important parameter for a good sensor, which is also investigated. The results were shown in Fig. 2F. No precipitated or floating particles were found even after being kept for one month. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of CDs displayed a negligible change, which indicated that the CDs earned good fluorescent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for the determination of Pb2+
ce:section.ce:para: Interestingly, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs was found to be effectively quenched by Pb2+, and which indicated that the obtained CDs could be utilized to develop a turn-off chemosensor for the detection of Pb2+ (Fig. 3 A). This phenomenon may be attributed to the chelating between Pb2+ and the hydroxyl group on the surface of CDs, and the chelating can facilitate non-radiative recombination of the excitons through an effective electron transfer process, leading to a remarkable decrease of the fluorescence intensity of CDs [39,44]. What’s more, some critical factors of experimental parameters such as the concentration of CDs, the incubation time, and pH of CDs solutions were further investigated for obtaining good parameters for the detection of Pb2+ based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism. In the optimization steps, the concentration of Pb2+ was set at 1.67μM. The (F0 −F)/F0 value was used as the signal for the determination of Pb2+, where F0 and F were the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of Pb2+, respectively.

The concentration of CDs was first optimized because lower concentrations of CDs resulted in higher sensitivity and a higher concentration will achieve a broader detection range. As shown in Fig. 3B, the concentration of CDs had significant influence on the detection of Pb2+. There was a maximal value of (F0 −F)/F0 at 0.008mgmL−1, while there was a comparable value of (F0 −F)/F0 at 0.024mgmL−1. In additionally, there was a certain correlation between the detection range of Pb2+ and the value of (F0 −F), and the enlargement of the detection range could be realized through the increase of the value of (F0 −F). In consideration of this view, the effect of the concentration of CDs on the (F0 −F) was also studied (Fig. 3C). It was found that the value of (F0 −F) increased with the increase of the concentration of CDs. When the concentration of CDs was above 0.024mgmL−1, the value of (F0 −F) had not obvious change. Based on the above experimental results and the comprehensive consideration of both the sensitivity and detection range, 0.024mgmL−1 of CDs was eventually employed as the optimized concentration for the determination of Pb2+ in the subsequent experiments.

The effect of the incubation time on the (F0 −F)/F0 value was shown in Fig. 3D. It revealed that the (F0 −F)/F0 value was stable after adding Pb2+ and changed imperceptibly within 10min which indicated the reaction between Pb2+ and CDs was rather fast and stable. Thus, the fluorescence intensity was stable and useful to rapidly determine Pb2+, which indicated that the chelating compound from CDs and Pb2+ was stable and the fluorescence intensity was stably kept for a longer time and which increased the accuracy of the data on account of the stability of chelating compound. Fig. 3E exhibited the influence of the pH on the value of (F0 −F)/F0. The value of (F0 −F)/F0 increased gradually to a maximum value with the increase of pH up to pH7.0 and later decreased. When the pH was lower than pH 7.0, some hydrogen ions in the solution could react with the hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs which made the fluorescence intensity of CDs decrease and may even reduce the reaction degree of Pb2+ and CDs. At more basic conditions, Pb2+ was easy to form Pb(OH)2 and so some of them cannot chelate with the CDs which would decrease the quenching effect and led to the decline of the (F0 −F)/F0 value. Hence, pH 7.0 was used as the optimal pH.

As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was obviously quenched in the concentration range of Pb2+ from 0.033 to 10μM. A good linear relationship was found between fluorescence response of CDs and Pb2+ concentration in the concentration range of 0.033–1.67μM (R2 =0.9996). The limit of detection was as low as 12.7nM based on 3σ/slope, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank signals (n=21). A comparison was made between present method and the existing methods, which was shown in Table 1 . As can be seen, the present study had better sensitivity for Pb2+ than most of the reported methods, and had similar sensitivity with that reported by Fu et al. and Han et al. [45,46], and the operation of present method was much simpler and easier. Jiang et al. has prepared N-doped CDs using glycerol the carbon source and ethylenediamine as a nitrogen doped molecule with a one step microwave irradiation method. The limit of detection for Pb2+ was 15nM, which was lower than that of present method [50]. All these indicated that present method was comparable to that of previously reported methods, and would be an excellent alternative method for monitoring Pb2+.

To take into account the reliability and stability of the proposed method, the intra-day, inter-day precision experiments and stability experiments were further performed. In the precision experiments, Pb2+ was spiked at three levels of 0.1, 0.83 and 1.67μM (n=6). The experimental results showed that the intra-day precisions for 0.1, 0.83 and 1.67μM were 1.71, 0.19, and 0.17% (RSD, n=6), and inter-day precisions for 0.1, 0.83 and 1.67μM were 1.42, 0.41, and 0.06% (RSD, n=6), respectively. Additionally, the fluorescence response of CDs to the two concentrations of Pb2+ at 0.1 and 0.83μM were recorded for 24h. It could be seen that the value of (F0 −F)/F0 exhibited no obvious changes within 24h in Fig. 5 . In a word, the experimental results showed that the stability of CDs was very good for the analysis of Pb2+.

As a chemosensor was concerned, high selectivity is another critical parameter. In present study, it was investigated by designing a series of experiments to spike Pb2+ at 0.1μM and 19 other kinds of metal ions at 0.5μM including Hg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, As3+, As5+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, Fe2+, Ag+, Na+ and K+, respectively. The influence of coexisting ions were shown in Fig. 6 (the blue bar portion). The results clearly demonstrated that only Pb2+ could efficiently quenched the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, while the quenching effect of the other metal ions was negligible. This discrimination effect may be ascribed to that Pb2+ had a stronger affinity and chelated fast with hydroxyl groups existing on the surface of CDs in comparison with other metal ions [3,39,44]. In another series of experiments, the mixtures of 0.1μM Pb2+ and 0.5μM of the above-mentioned metal ions were added into the CDs solution, and then the fluorescence responses of CDs (Fig. 6, the red bars) were recorded. It was not hard to find that the other coexisting metal ions displayed no obviously interference compared to the efficiently quenching effect of Pb2+, which forcefully suggested that the as-prepared CDs had high selectivity toward Pb2+ over the other relevant metal ions.

The developed chemosensor has shown high sensitivity and selectivity towards Pb2+, and in order to valid the applicability of the chemosensor, five real water samples were utilized to evaluate the feasibility and reliability of the proposed method. The blank samples and spiked samples were analyzed by the developed chemosensor. The results showed that high concentration of Pb2+ in the blank sample from the factory samples, and no Pb2+ was found in the blank samples of other water samples. In the spiked experiments, the sample from factory was diluted by 30 times for use. The experimental results of the blank and spiked real water samples were demonstrated in Table 2 . It could be clearly seen that the spiked recoveries were in the range of 96.5–102% and the RSD was lower than 3.63%. The results revealed that the CDs as the chemosensor resulted in high sensitivity for Pb2+ and this developed method would be competitive and could be used as an alternative tool for the determination of Pb2+ in practical applications.

In present study, a new water-soluble fluorescent CD was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method from chocolate, which was used to develop a novel chemosensor for the rapid detection of Pb2+ based on the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The results showed that the chemosensor owned the advantages of simplicity, high sensitivity and selectivity, low cost and without any other chemical modification. The fluorescence intensity of obtained CDs could be significantly quenched by Pb2+, and good linear relationship was achieved between the fluorescence response of CDs and the concentration of Pb2+ in the range from 0.033μM to 1.67μM. The limit of detection was 12.7nM under the optimized conditions. The developed method was validated with five real water samples and satisfied spiked recoveries were achieved. It is believed that the developed chemosensor with as-prepared CDs from chocolate demonstrates good merits such as low cost, rapidness, high sensitivity and selectivity, which will have great potential in biological and environmental application in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors appreciate financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21377167)

Gold nanoparticle probes for the detection of mercury, lead and copper ions

Colorimetric detection of lead(II) based on silver nanoparticles capped with iminodiacetic acid
Detection of lead(II) with a turn-on fluorescent biosensor based on energy transfer from CdSe/ZnS quantum dots to graphene oxide
Dithizone functionalized CdSe/CdS quantum dots as turn-on fluorescent probe for ultrasensitive detection of lead ion
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a turn-off fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Fast one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots by pyrolyzing ethanolamine
A novel one-pot route for large-scale preparation of highly photoluminescent carbon quantum dots powders
Bioimaging based on fluorescent carbon dots
Multifunctional water-soluble luminescent carbon dots for imaging and Hg2+ sensing
A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature
Synthesis of a carbon-dot-based photoluminescent probe for selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ in water and living cells
Carbon dots as fluorescent probes for off–on detection of Cu2+ and l-cysteine in aqueous solution
Preparation of highly photoluminescent sulfur-doped carbon dots for Fe(III) detection
A carbon dots-based fluorescence turn-on method for DNA determination
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Low-cost synthesis of carbon nanodots from natural products used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of ferrum(III) ions in lake water
One-step synthesis of fluorescent hydroxyls-coated carbon dots with hydrothermal reaction and its application to optical sensing of metal ions
Synthesis of biocompatible and highly photoluminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from lime: analytical applications and optimization using response surface methodology
Fluorescent carbon nanodots for sensitive and selective detection of tannic acid in wines
Convenient and sensitive detection of norfloxacin with fluorescent carbon dots
A green and facile approach for the synthesis of water soluble fluorescent carbon dots from banana juice
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots: a facile and general preparation method photoluminescence investigation, and imaging applications
One-pot electrochemical synthesis of functionalized fluorescent carbon dots and their selective sensing for mercury ion
Turn-on fluorescence detection of lead ions based on accelerated leaching of gold nanoparticles on the surface of graphene
Ultrasensitive photoelectrochemical sensing of Pb2+ based on allosteric transition of G-Quadruplex DNAzyme
Designing bifunctionalized gold nanoparticle for colorimetric detection of Pb2+ under physiological condition
Colorimetric detection of heavy metal ions using label-free gold nanoparticles and alkanethiols

N-doped carbon dots synthesized by rapid microwave irradiation as highly fluorescent probes for Pb2+ detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yongli Liu received her Master Degree in Chemical Engineering from Zhengzhou University in 2014. She is currently a PhD student majoring in Chemistry at the Faculty of Science, China University of Petroleum (Beijing). Her research focuses on the synthesis and surface functionalization of carbon quantum dots and their application in the detection of heavy metal ions and environmental pollutants.

Qingxiang Zhou obtained PhD degree in Environmental Science from Research Center for Eco-Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China in 2004. He joined Henan Normal University in 1999. Currently, he is a professor of environmental science at College of Geosciences, China University of Petroleum (Beijing). He has published more than 140 research papers in international peer reviewed journals and achieved eight Scholar Awards. His area of research includes environmental science, analytical chemistry, and environmental nanomaterials and its applications.

Jing Li obtained her bachelor degree in Environmental Science from North West Agriculture and Forestry University. Currently she is a PhD student majoring in Chemistry at the Faculty of Science, China University of Petroleum (Beijing). Her research interests include the removal and adsorption mechanism of environmental pollutants, magnetic nanomaterials, carbon dots and its application.

Man Lei received her bachelor degree in Environmental Science from China University of Petroleum (Beijing) in 2014 and is currently a graduate student majoring in Environmental Science at College of Geosciences at the same institution. Her research interests include the synthesis and modification of nanoscale zero-valent iron materials and their application in the adsorption of environmental pollutants.

Xiuyi Yan obtained her PhD degree in Environmental Science from Tohoku University of Japan in 2007. She is an associate professor of Environmental Science at China University of Petroleum (Beijing), and her research interests include the application of supercritical fluid technology in the field of environment and energy.
